trivia_bullets.text: Kin-Tree Invocation is an Abzan Houses uncommon sorcery ({B}{G}). In Abzan culture, each family tends a kin-tree — a fruit-bearing tree beneath which ancestors are buried without a casket, their spirits dwelling in the tree. Those bonded to the tree can summon ancestor spirits to fight. The card mechanically represents this: it creates a Spirit Warrior token scaled to the toughness of the Abzan's most enduring living fighter. [Wizards: Planeswalker's Guide to Khans of Tarkir, Part 1], [Scryfall]

Creates an X/X black-green Spirit Warrior token where X = the greatest toughness among creatures you control. Toughness-as-power-source reflects Abzan's endurance identity. Direct synergy with Outlast: Outlasted creatures accumulate +1/+1 counters and grow in toughness, feeding larger invocation tokens each cast. [Scryfall], [Wizards: Mechanics of Khans of Tarkir]

"The passing years add new rings to the tree's trunk, bolstering the spirits that dwell within." [Scryfall] — The rings-of-a-tree metaphor maps directly to the game mechanic: each year (each counter, each Outlast activation) adds to the spiritual reserve that Kin-Tree Invocation draws upon. Flavor-to-function alignment is tight.

X is locked in at resolution; the token's stats don't change if toughness later shifts. High-toughness creatures like Ivorytusk Fortress (5/7) or Doran, the Siege Tower create large token baselines. EDHREC reports 2,138 Commander decks include Kin-Tree Invocation, rank 18,844 — present in dedicated Abzan toughness-matters builds. [EDHREC: Kin-Tree Invocation], [Scryfall]

Illustrated by Ryan Alexander Lee. The card's frontmatter contained a manual_review_reason flagging 'No reference image found via scryfall for set Khans of Tarkir' during initial prep, though the reference_image_source_url was subsequently resolved correctly from Scryfall. The card record is in order. [Scryfall], [card frontmatter]
